analysis_scope: CVEs whose underlying bug class may still affect a Rust reimplementation of libarchive, with emphasis on non-memory-corruption issues plus numeric, parser-state, and bounds-validation bugs that remain relevant even when Rust removes the original C memory-unsafety impact

included_issue_classes: parser bugs that break forward progress or permit unbounded work
filesystem extraction bugs involving path resolution, link handling, permissions, or sandbox escape
numeric-validation bugs where unchecked arithmetic, signedness, or allocation math can still cause incorrect behavior, panic, denial of service, or invalid archive handling in safe Rust
parser-state and bounds-validation bugs where malformed headers, truncation, cursor movement, window sizes, or oversized metadata still need explicit rejection in a Rust parser
excluded_issue_classes: heap or stack overflows, out-of-bounds reads or writes, use-after-free, double-free, and similar memory-corruption issues when the underlying bug does not also expose a Rust-relevant validation or arithmetic invariant
straight NULL-dereference cases that are largely artifacts of C allocation and pointer handling
tool-only presentation bugs that do not materially change core library or extraction semantics
notes: Some selected CVEs were triggered through bsdtar or bsdcpio, but were kept when the underlying issue maps to parser or extraction semantics that a Rust port would still need to preserve safely.

Numeric-validation CVEs are retained when the root cause is unchecked arithmetic, signedness, or size validation that a Rust port must still handle explicitly, even if the original C impact escalated into memory corruption.

Some parser-validation CVEs were originally reported as out-of-bounds reads, but were kept because the Rust-relevant carryover is malformed-header rejection, cursor or window invariant enforcement, or explicit size caps.

records.component_scope: 32-bit zisofs block-pointer allocation in the ISO9660 reader
records.rust_port_relevance: The original C impact was a heap overflow, but the bug class that carries into Rust is unchecked allocation arithmetic on attacker-controlled block counts, especially on 32-bit targets.
required_controls: use checked multiplication and addition when sizing zisofs block-pointer tables
reject images whose derived allocation exceeds the target architecture's addressable size
test 32-bit builds explicitly for size and count overflow behavior

source_evidence.description: A flaw was found in libarchive. On 32-bit systems, an integer overflow vulnerability exists in the zisofs block pointer allocation logic. A remote attacker can exploit this by providing a specially crafted ISO9660 image, which can lead to a heap buffer overflow. This could potentially allow for arbitrary code execution on the affected system.

records.component_scope: zisofs decompression in the ISO9660 reader
records.rust_port_relevance: Rust memory safety does not validate archive-controlled numeric fields. The port still needs checked validation of `pz_log2_bs` and bounded allocation math to avoid denial of service or panic paths.
required_controls: validate `pz_log2_bs` against the format's legal range
use checked shift and size computations for block sizing
cap allocation requests derived from archive metadata

source_evidence.description: A flaw was found in libarchive. An Undefined Behavior vulnerability exists in the zisofs decompression logic, caused by improper validation of a field (`pz_log2_bs`) read from ISO9660 Rock Ridge extensions. A remote attacker can exploit this by supplying a specially crafted ISO file. This can lead to incorrect memory allocation and potential application crashes, resulting in a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

records.component_scope: RAR LZSS window handling across compression-method transitions
records.rust_port_relevance: The C manifestation was an out-of-bounds read, but the Rust-relevant issue is parser-state validation: the decoder must restore or resize the LZSS window correctly when compression methods change, or later slice access will panic or produce invalid output.
required_controls: revalidate LZSS window size after every compression-method transition
bind filter and copy operations to the active window bounds before decoding
treat inconsistent decoder state transitions as hard parse failures

source_evidence.description: A flaw was found in libarchive. This heap out-of-bounds read vulnerability exists in the RAR archive processing logic due to improper validation of the LZSS sliding window size after transitions between compression methods. A remote attacker can exploit this by providing a specially crafted RAR archive, leading to the disclosure of sensitive heap memory information without requiring authentication or user interaction.

records.rust_port_relevance: Rust does not guarantee forward progress. A decompressor state machine can still spin forever unless the port explicitly detects no-progress iterations and rejects hostile inputs.
required_controls: detect iterations that consume no input and produce no output
bound decompressor work relative to consumed bytes
treat checksum-valid archives as still untrusted for progress guarantees

source_evidence.description: A flaw was identified in the RAR5 archive decompression logic of the libarchive library, specifically within the archive_read_data() processing path. When a specially crafted RAR5 archive is processed, the decompression routine may enter a state where internal logic prevents forward progress. This condition results in an infinite loop that continuously consumes CPU resources. Because the archive passes checksum validation and appears structurally valid, affected applications cannot detect the issue before processing. This can allow attackers to cause persistent denial-of-service conditions in services that automatically process archives.

records.rust_port_relevance: A Rust port still needs explicit overflow checks on very large advertised content lengths. Without them, the likely Rust manifestation is panic, invalid allocation sizing, excessive skipping, or denial of service rather than raw memory corruption.
required_controls: reject WARC content lengths that exceed representable internal limits
use checked arithmetic when adding framing overhead to claimed body sizes
cap read, skip, and allocation paths derived from WARC length metadata

source_evidence.description: A vulnerability has been identified in the libarchive library. This flaw involves an integer overflow that can be triggered when processing a Web Archive (WARC) file that claims to have more than INT64_MAX - 4 content bytes. An attacker could craft a malicious WARC archive to induce this overflow, potentially leading to unpredictable program behavior, memory corruption, or a denial-of-service condition within applications that process such archives using libarchive. This bug affects libarchive versions prior to 3.8.0.

records.rust_port_relevance: Even in Rust, a decoder must reject filter blocks that exceed the active LZSS window. Otherwise malformed archives can still drive invalid slice ranges, panics, or incorrect decompression behavior.
required_controls: check filter-block size against the currently active LZSS window before use
reject archives that require copying or reading beyond the decoder window
keep filter execution state synchronized with window-size updates

source_evidence.description: A vulnerability has been identified in the libarchive library. This flaw can lead to a heap buffer over-read due to the size of a filter block potentially exceeding the Lempel-Ziv-Storer-Schieber (LZSS) window. This means the library may attempt to read beyond the allocated memory buffer, which can result in unpredictable program behavior, crashes (denial of service), or the disclosure of sensitive information from adjacent memory regions.

records.rust_port_relevance: The original exploit chain ended in a double-free, but the Rust-relevant carryover is unchecked seek or cursor arithmetic in attacker-controlled RAR metadata. That still needs validation to avoid invalid state transitions and denial of service.
required_controls: use checked arithmetic for seek offsets and compressed-data extents
reject entries whose derived seek positions wrap or move backward unexpectedly
keep reader state transitions single-owner and monotonic across seek calculations

source_evidence.description: A vulnerability has been identified in the libarchive library, specifically within the archive_read_format_rar_seek_data() function. This flaw involves an integer overflow that can ultimately lead to a double-free condition. Exploiting a double-free vulnerability can result in memory corruption, enabling an attacker to execute arbitrary code or cause a denial-of-service condition.

records.component_scope: GNU longlink truncation handling in the TAR reader
records.rust_port_relevance: The key carryover is truncation-aware string parsing. A Rust implementation still needs to reject longlink metadata that ends mid-name instead of building inconsistent path state or panicking on later bounds checks.
required_controls: treat truncated GNU longlink payloads as malformed headers
require complete name termination before longlink material is consumed
keep path-buffer length bookkeeping synchronized with truncated input

source_evidence.description: libarchive through 3.7.7 has a heap-based buffer over-read in header_gnu_longlink in archive_read_support_format_tar.c via a TAR archive because it mishandles truncation in the middle of a GNU long linkname.

records.rust_port_relevance: This is fundamentally a decoder-cursor invariant problem. Rust would turn the same bug into slice-range panics or forced error paths unless the filter keeps `src` and `dst` movement within validated bounds.
required_controls: validate every delta-filter cursor move against the destination window
reject filters whose computed `src` position runs past `dst`
keep filter-state transitions explicit and bounds-checked

source_evidence.description: execute_filter_delta in archive_read_support_format_rar.c in libarchive before 3.7.5 allows out-of-bounds access via a crafted archive file because src can move beyond dst.

records.rust_port_relevance: Like the delta-filter case, the issue is decoder-state validation rather than raw pointer management. The Rust port still needs strict cursor bounds for audio-filter execution.
required_controls: validate audio-filter cursor updates before each read or write
forbid filter state that allows `src` to advance beyond `dst`
convert inconsistent filter metadata into a hard decode error

source_evidence.description: execute_filter_audio in archive_read_support_format_rar.c in libarchive before 3.7.5 allows out-of-bounds access via a crafted archive file because src can move beyond dst.

records.component_scope: ZIP empty-name handling with mac-ext enabled
records.rust_port_relevance: Empty entry names are a parser validation edge case, not a C-only artifact. The Rust port still needs to define and enforce how empty names interact with mac-ext handling and central-directory parsing.
required_controls: reject or explicitly special-case empty ZIP entry names before mac-ext processing
keep name-length checks consistent between local and central directory parsing
avoid deriving path or metadata state from zero-length names without an explicit rule

source_evidence.description: Libarchive before 3.7.4 allows name out-of-bounds access when a ZIP archive has an empty-name file and mac-ext is enabled. This occurs in slurp_central_directory in archive_read_support_format_zip.c.

records.component_scope: write-to-disk path handling and permission application
records.rust_port_relevance: This is a process-global state bug, not a pointer bug. A Rust port can still recreate it if it mutates `umask()` while extracting or relies on ambient process state instead of explicit permissions.
required_controls: avoid temporary process-wide `umask()` changes during extraction
create directories with explicit modes and repair permissions without global side effects
document or enforce thread-safety expectations for disk-write APIs

source_evidence.description: Libarchive through 3.6.2 can cause directories to have world-writable permissions. The umask() call inside archive_write_disk_posix.c changes the umask of the whole process for a very short period of time; a race condition with another thread can lead to a permanent umask 0 setting. Such a race condition could lead to implicit directory creation with permissions 0777 (without the sticky bit), which means that any low-privileged local user can delete and rename files inside those directories.

records.component_scope: link resolution while applying metadata during extraction
records.rust_port_relevance: A safe language does not solve TOCTOU or symlink-target confusion. The extraction engine still needs descriptor-based updates and strict containment checks so metadata changes cannot escape the extraction root.
required_controls: apply metadata to opened file descriptors instead of re-resolving paths
re-check containment after filesystem mutations
refuse symlink and hardlink targets that escape the extraction root

source_evidence.description: An improper link resolution flaw can occur while extracting an archive leading to changing modes, times, access control lists, and flags of a file outside of the archive. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to gain more privileges in a system.

records.rust_port_relevance: ACL handling has the same containment problem as mode and timestamp updates: the port must not follow links or re-resolve paths in a way that lets attacker-controlled archive entries retarget privilege changes.
required_controls: treat ACL application as a privileged metadata operation requiring containment checks
avoid following links when setting ACLs
bind ACL updates to the extracted object rather than a later pathname lookup

source_evidence.description: An improper link resolution flaw while extracting an archive can lead to changing the access control list (ACL) of the target of the link. An attacker may provide a malicious archive to a victim user, who would trigger this flaw when trying to extract the archive. A local attacker may use this flaw to change the ACL of a file on the system and gain more privileges.

records.rust_port_relevance: The decoder still needs to reject structurally impossible headers, such as zero-length RAR5 headers, before state is advanced. In Rust this would otherwise become a panic or malformed-state decode failure rather than a SIGSEGV.
required_controls: enforce minimum nonzero header-size constraints before header dispatch
reject corrupted headers before any derived cursor movement or allocation
require header length fields to fit the remaining input window

source_evidence.description: archive_read_support_format_rar5.c in libarchive before 3.4.2 attempts to unpack a RAR5 file with an invalid or corrupted header (such as a header size of zero), leading to a SIGSEGV or possibly unspecified other impact.

records.rust_port_relevance: This is a parser-state termination bug. A Rust port still needs explicit loop invariants and cycle or repetition detection when following continuation records.
required_controls: track visited continuation locations or bound the chain length
guarantee progress on every Rock Ridge parsing step
fail closed when a continuation record repeats or regresses

source_evidence.description: libarchive version commit 5a98dcf8a86364b3c2c469c85b93647dfb139961 onwards (version v2.8.0 onwards) contains a CWE-835: Loop with Unreachable Exit Condition ('Infinite Loop') vulnerability in ISO9660 parser, archive_read_support_format_iso9660.c, read_CE()/parse_rockridge() that can result in DoS by infinite loop. This attack appears to be exploitable via the victim opening a specially crafted ISO9660 file.

records.component_scope: WARC parser record and body handling
records.rust_port_relevance: Rust does not prevent decompression bombs or tiny-input huge-output abuse. The WARC reader still needs explicit caps on claimed lengths, emitted data, and work performed per record.
required_controls: cap WARC record sizes and output growth
reject implausible body lengths before streaming work begins
budget CPU and disk work for records that expand attacker-controlled content

source_evidence.description: libarchive version commit 9693801580c0cf7c70e862d305270a16b52826a7 onwards (release v3.2.0 onwards) contains a CWE-20: Improper Input Validation vulnerability in WARC parser - libarchive/archive_read_support_format_warc.c, _warc_read() that can result in DoS - quasi-infinite run time and disk usage from tiny file. This attack appear to be exploitable via the victim must open a specially crafted WARC file.

records.rust_port_relevance: The relevant invariant is header-consistency checking. A Rust port still needs to reject 7zip headers that repeat EmptyStream attributes in structurally invalid ways before any indexing or table expansion occurs.
required_controls: validate that each 7zip stream attribute appears only in legal combinations
bound EmptyStream-derived indexing against the file count
treat repeated or contradictory header attributes as malformed input

source_evidence.description: The read_Header function in archive_read_support_format_7zip.c in libarchive 3.2.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (out-of-bounds read) via multiple EmptyStream attributes in a header in a 7zip archive.

records.rust_port_relevance: This is a line-size bookkeeping bug. A Rust port still needs explicit limits and cursor accounting when extending read-ahead buffers for mtree input, or malformed long lines can still trigger panics or excessive work.
required_controls: track current line length separately from total read-ahead size
enforce maximum line sizes before extending the lookahead buffer
recompute parser cursors after read-ahead growth instead of assuming prior offsets stay valid

source_evidence.description: The mtree bidder in libarchive 3.2.1 does not keep track of line sizes when extending the read-ahead, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted file, which triggers an invalid read in the (1) detect_form or (2) bid_entry function in libarchive/archive_read_support_format_mtree.c.

records.rust_port_relevance: Recursion and expansion limits are semantic resource controls. A Rust port can still be vulnerable to nested-filter bombs unless it enforces depth and output budgets.
required_controls: limit recursive filter nesting depth
cap total decompressed output and intermediate expansion
surface hard failure instead of continuing after budget exhaustion

source_evidence.description: libarchive before 3.2.0 does not limit the number of recursive decompressions, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (memory consumption and application crash) via a crafted gzip file.

records.rust_port_relevance: If the Rust port includes archive writing, filename-length verification is still a numeric-validation problem. Rust removes the buffer overflow primitive, but not incorrect width calculations or impossible output layouts.
required_controls: use checked length accounting for ISO9660 path components and directory records
reject filenames whose encoded size exceeds format limits before buffer or record construction
avoid lossy conversions between host lengths and on-disk field widths

source_evidence.description: Integer overflow in the ISO9660 writer in libarchive before 3.2.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) or execute arbitrary code via vectors related to verifying filename lengths when writing an ISO9660 archive, which trigger a buffer overflow.

records.component_scope: sandboxed extraction of hardlink entries with data
records.rust_port_relevance: This is an extraction-policy bug. Even in Rust, hardlink handling can break sandboxing if link targets, data writes, and containment checks are not modeled explicitly.
required_controls: treat hardlink entries with data as security-sensitive special cases
never allow sandbox escape through link target resolution
perform path containment and overwrite policy checks before any data write

source_evidence.description: The sandboxing code in libarchive 3.2.0 and earlier mishandles hardlink archive entries of non-zero data size, which might allow remote attackers to write to arbitrary files via a crafted archive file.

records.component_scope: CPIO symlink-size validation in header parsing
records.rust_port_relevance: Oversized symlink targets are still a parser and resource-validation problem in Rust. The port needs explicit bounds on link-target length and must reject impossible CPIO metadata before attempting to materialize or normalize the link name.
required_controls: cap CPIO symlink target sizes before allocating or decoding the link body
reject entries whose declared symlink length exceeds the remaining archive data
treat oversized or truncated symlink metadata as a hard parse error

source_evidence.description: The archive_read_format_cpio_read_header function in archive_read_support_format_cpio.c in libarchive before 3.2.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a CPIO archive with a large symlink.

records.component_scope: ISO parser offset and length calculations
records.rust_port_relevance: Rust still needs checked integer arithmetic for archive-controlled lengths and offsets. Without that, the port can panic, mis-parse, or accept impossible structures even if it never corrupts memory.
required_controls: use checked add, mul, and shift operations for ISO offsets and sizes
reject records whose claimed layout cannot fit within the input
treat arithmetic overflow as a parse error, not a recoverable condition

source_evidence.description: Integer overflow in the ISO parser in libarchive before 3.2.1 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (application crash) via a crafted ISO file.

records.rust_port_relevance: Large attacker-controlled substream counts still require checked multiplication and bounded allocation strategy in Rust. Otherwise the port can panic, mis-size vectors, or accept impossible 7zip metadata.
required_controls: cap the number of substreams permitted by a single folder or entry
use checked multiplication when sizing per-substream tables
reject metadata whose aggregate decoded structure cannot fit in memory or the input model

source_evidence.description: Integer overflow in the read_SubStreamsInfo function in archive_read_support_format_7zip.c in libarchive before 3.2.1 allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code via a 7zip file with a large number of substreams, which triggers a heap-based buffer overflow.

records.rust_port_relevance: Skipping through tar payloads still depends on checked size arithmetic and monotonic cursor movement. In Rust the likely failure mode is panic or a malformed-stream denial of service, but the invariant is the same.
required_controls: use checked arithmetic for skip amounts and block alignment calculations
reject tar entries whose claimed size would wrap or exceed the remaining stream
preserve monotonic reader-position invariants across skip operations

source_evidence.description: Integer overflow in the archive_read_format_tar_skip function in archive_read_support_format_tar.c in libarchive before 3.2.0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted tar file.

records.rust_port_relevance: Shift-count and bit-width validation remain necessary in Rust. A hostile archive can still drive panic or misclassification if the filter setup trusts invalid bit parameters.
required_controls: validate shift counts before any bit operation
bound compression parameters to the format's legal range
convert malformed bidder state into an early parse error

source_evidence.description: The compress_bidder_init function in archive_read_support_filter_compress.c in libarchive before 3.2.0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted tar file, which triggers an invalid left shift.

records.rust_port_relevance: Timestamp conversions and boundary helpers are still vulnerable to arithmetic mistakes in safe Rust. The port must explicitly reject out-of-range time values instead of relying on ambient integer behavior.
required_controls: use checked conversions between archive timestamps and host time types
reject mtree values outside representable bounds
keep min and max helper logic free of wraparound assumptions

source_evidence.description: Multiple integer overflows in the (1) get_time_t_max and (2) get_time_t_min functions in archive_read_support_format_mtree.c in libarchive before 3.2.0 allow remote attackers to have unspecified impact via a crafted mtree file, which triggers undefined behavior.

records.component_scope: ISO directory traversal surfaced through bsdtar
records.rust_port_relevance: The issue is a logical cycle in archive graph traversal. A Rust port still needs visited-node tracking or depth guards when walking directory relationships reconstructed from archive metadata.
required_controls: detect self-referential and cyclic directory structures
bound directory walk depth and node revisits
fail closed when parent-child relationships are inconsistent

source_evidence.description: bsdtar in libarchive before 3.2.0 allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (infinite loop) via an ISO with a directory that is a member of itself.

records.rust_port_relevance: Signed and unsigned offset math is still a correctness hazard in Rust. The port needs explicit range checks so hostile metadata cannot create invalid cursor movement or panic conditions.
required_controls: normalize signed versus unsigned lengths before offset arithmetic
check every cursor advance against the available buffer
reject malformed ZIP extra fields before partial parsing mutates state

source_evidence.description: The process_extra function in libarchive before 3.2.0 uses the size field and a signed number in an offset, which allows remote attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via a crafted zip file.

records.rust_port_relevance: Path traversal is independent of implementation language. Any Rust extraction helper must sanitize absolute paths and enforce an extraction root before touching the filesystem.
required_controls: reject absolute paths unless an explicit unsafe mode is enabled
canonicalize and enforce extraction-root containment for every entry
separate archive path semantics from host filesystem path joins

source_evidence.description: Absolute path traversal vulnerability in bsdcpio in libarchive 3.1.2 and earlier allows remote attackers to write to arbitrary files via a full pathname in an archive.

records.component_scope: ZIP writer signed and unsigned size conversion
records.rust_port_relevance: Cross-width size conversion bugs still matter in Rust, especially when translating `usize` or `u64` host sizes into ZIP format fields with narrower signed or unsigned representations.
required_controls: use checked conversions when writing ZIP field sizes and offsets
reject host-side lengths that cannot be represented in the target ZIP encoding
test writer behavior on both 32-bit and 64-bit platforms

source_evidence.description: Integer signedness error in the archive_write_zip_data function in archive_write_set_format_zip.c in libarchive 3.1.2 and earlier, when running on 64-bit machines, allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (crash) via unspecified vectors, which triggers an improper conversion between unsigned and signed types, leading to a buffer overflow.

records.rust_port_relevance: Malformed-header handling still needs a strict progress rule. A Rust parser can loop forever on EOF or invalid length records unless every iteration either consumes input or returns an error.
required_controls: ensure PAX parsing advances the cursor or aborts
treat EOF inside an extended header as a hard parse failure
bound the number of malformed-record recovery attempts

source_evidence.description: archive_read_support_format_tar.c in libarchive before 2.2.4 allows user-assisted remote attackers to cause a denial of service (infinite loop) via (1) an end-of-file condition within a pax extension header or (2) a malformed pax extension header in an (a) PAX or a (b) TAR archive.

records.component_scope: generic skip logic near physical end of archive
records.rust_port_relevance: Skipping and streaming APIs need explicit EOF invariants. Rust will not prevent an implementation from repeatedly requesting impossible skips and spinning on empty reads.
required_controls: reject skip requests that extend past known physical EOF
treat repeated zero-byte reads during a skip as fatal
maintain monotonic stream-position invariants in the reader core

source_evidence.description: The libarchive library in FreeBSD 6-STABLE after 2006-09-05 and before 2006-11-08 allows context-dependent attackers to cause a denial of service (CPU consumption) via a malformed archive that causes libarchive to skip a region past the actual end of the archive, which triggers an infinite loop that attempts to read more data.
